parentQuestion: Cones come in three types, each sensitive to different wavelengths of light.

scaffolds.q: Cones are the photoreceptors responsible for color vision.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! Cones detect color; rods only detect brightness (achromatic).

scaffolds.q: Different colors have different wavelengths of light.
scaffolds.explain: Correct! Red = long wavelength (~700nm), blue = short wavelength (~450nm).

scaffolds.q: To perceive different colors, you need photoreceptors with different sensitivities.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! One photoreceptor type can't distinguish wavelengths - it just counts photons.

scaffolds.q: Humans have THREE types of cones: S, M, and L.
scaffolds.explain: Correct! S = short wavelength (blue), M = medium (green), L = long (red).

scaffolds.q: Each cone type contains a different photopigment (opsin) with different spectral sensitivity.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! Different opsins absorb maximally at different wavelengths.

scaffolds.q: Color perception comes from COMPARING activation across the three cone types.
scaffolds.explain: Correct! The brain interprets the RATIO of S:M:L activation as a specific color.

scaffolds.q: This is called 'trichromatic' vision - three color channels.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! Tri- = three. Three cone types, millions of perceivable colors.

scaffolds.q: Three cone types with different wavelength sensitivities enable color vision.
scaffolds.explain: Correct! S-cones (blue), M-cones (green), L-cones (red) together create full color perception.

scaffolds.q: The answer is TRUE - cones come in three types with different wavelength sensitivities.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! Trichromatic theory of color vision: S, M, and L cones with different opsins, each maximally sensitive to short, medium, or long wavelengths respectively.
